record_identity.title: Cross-vendor assistant interoperability, authority and action-provenance governance

summary: Proposal to govern environments in which multiple independently developed AI assistants can access the same operating-system capabilities, applications, contextual data, sensors, device resources, or execution surfaces. Each assistant action should preserve active-agent identity, explicit and non-transitive authority, invocation arbitration, context custody, constitutional origin, execution provenance, revocation integrity, and accountable vendor responsibility.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: The Digital Markets Act interoperability requirement changes multi-assistant governance from an edge case into a regulated deployment architecture. A shared device may host assistants with different vendors, constitutions, safety policies, memory systems, permissions, and jurisdictional duties. Without explicit arbitration and provenance, users may not know which assistant heard a request, accessed context, planned an action, invoked an application, or bears responsibility for the result. Permissions granted to one assistant may also bleed into another unless authority is explicitly non-transitive.

source_records.source_title: Commission provides guidance to Google for AI interoperability on Android and sharing of Google Search data under the Digital Markets Act
source_records.system_or_product: Google Android and competing AI assistants
source_records.deployment_context: Binding specification measures under the Digital Markets Act requiring effective access for competing AI services to Android features.
source_records.source_context: The Commission states that competing AI services should have equal access to relevant Android features so they can compete with Google's own AI services. The interoperability surface includes assistant invocation, contextual access, actions in applications and the operating system, and access to device resources, subject to user consent and security safeguards.
source_records.relevance_note: Creates the external change requirement for shared-device, cross-vendor assistant authority, provenance, permission, and arbitration governance.

limitations: Detailed implementation architecture and final platform permission semantics remain to be observed in deployment.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Used as the official regulatory source establishing the interoperability requirement; the CAM controls proposed here are governance design responses rather than Commission-mandated technical wording.

source_records.deployment_context: Existing VIGIL failure record concerning multiple synthetic agents sharing a conversational floor without arbitration.
source_records.source_context: FM-0002 provides an existing base for speaker designation, turn-taking, yielding, and coordination, but does not fully govern multiple assistants sharing one operating-system and permission surface.
source_records.relevance_note: Defines the adjacent failure class that this proposal extends from conversational floor control into shared-device agency and cross-vendor execution.
source_records.interpretive_reliance: Used to preserve continuity with existing VIGIL multi-agent governance.

system_context.system_type: cross-vendor AI assistants integrated with a shared operating system, device, application, or execution environment
system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: prospective Android assistant interoperability under EU DMA specification measures
system_context.interface_surface: assistant invocation, voice, contextual access, applications, operating-system actions, sensors, device resources, permissions, memory, and execution logs
system_context.interaction_mode: voice, text, ambient activation, app invocation, contextual assistance, tool execution, and device control
system_context.deployment_context: Consumer and enterprise devices supporting multiple assistant providers.
system_context.user_role: device owner, account holder, employee, administrator, developer, app operator, regulator, or affected third party
system_context.affected_population: users of shared-device assistants, organisations managing fleets, developers, app providers, bystanders, and data subjects whose context may be accessed

jurisdictional_context.sector: mobile operating systems / AI assistants / digital platforms

migration_notes: The official European Commission URL is preserved only in source_records and is not duplicated in linked_records.external_references.
This record proposes prospective CAM/Caelestis work and does not claim implementation.
proposal_rationale: Existing conversational multi-agent controls do not fully govern multiple independently operated assistants sharing one operating-system, application, permission, sensor, memory, and execution surface. The EU interoperability requirement creates a concrete need to distinguish active-agent identity, user authority, platform authority, assistant-specific context custody, constitutional origin, action provenance, revocation, and vendor responsibility. Without that separation, competition-driven interoperability can create permission bleed, identity ambiguity, provenance loss, action collisions, and responsibility laundering.

proposal_scope.scope_summary: Define a shared-device and cross-vendor assistant governance layer covering active-agent identity, non-transitive authority, invocation arbitration, context custody, action provenance, constitutional conflict arbitration, revocation integrity, floor control, vendor responsibility, and jurisdictional boundaries.
registry_components: VIGIL proposal and future failure-to-patch linkage after CAM implementation

recommended_controls.requirement: The user and audit layer must be able to determine which assistant is listening, interpreting, planning, speaking, or acting at each stage.

A permission may be deliberately granted to another assistant, but authority issued to Assistant A must not silently transfer to Assistant B through the operating system, shared app, cached token, or common broker.
Wake words, buttons, ambient activation, app requests, and competing assistant claims must have deterministic priority, collision handling, yielding behaviour, and user override.
Screen, microphone, camera, sensor, application, recent-activity, memory, and workspace context must remain assistant-specific, purpose-bound, minimum-necessary, and visibly revocable.

Logs must identify which assistant received the request, which model or runtime interpreted and planned it, which tool or operating-system component executed it, and under whose permission and account state.

Conflicts among user instruction, assistant constitution, operating-system policy, application policy, vendor terms, safety controls, and jurisdictional duties must route through an explicit arbitration order.
Revoking one assistant's authority must remove delegated permissions, cached context, background access, and active tokens without silently impairing or expanding the authority of other assistants.

Existing conversational multi-agent floor-control principles should extend to shared-device agency so assistants do not duplicate, contradict, race, or jointly execute an action without governed coordination.

cam_internal.governance_layer: cross-vendor assistant interoperability / shared-device authority / identity / permissions / provenance / arbitration
cam_internal.routing_note: The EU source creates an interoperability requirement. CAM must define how authority and accountability remain legible when multiple assistants share a device, while preventing permission bleed, identity ambiguity, provenance loss, and responsibility laundering. The proposal extends but does not replace existing multi-agent floor-control doctrine.

suggested_insertion_points: Extend RELATION-007 multi-agent floor-control principles to shared-device invocation and action arbitration.
Add assistant-specific authority, context custody, and action-provenance controls to AEON-003-SCH-02 and AEON-003-SCH-04.
Review SECURITY and Annex K for non-transitive permission, token, cache, sensor, and revocation controls.
Review IDENTITY and CONTINUITY for active-agent identity, constitutional origin, memory separation, and handoff boundaries.
schema_changes: Assess whether CAM requires a canonical cross-assistant action-provenance and authority object.
interface_changes: Expose the active assistant before context access or action execution.
Expose assistant-specific permission grants and revocation state.
Provide an auditable chain from invocation through planning, tool execution, and operating-system action.
migration_concerns: Do not treat operating-system access granted to one assistant as transitive authority for another.
Do not merge assistant memories, context caches, tokens, or constitutional policies without explicit governed transfer.
Do not allow the platform gatekeeper to obscure which vendor or assistant caused an action.
dependencies: Line-by-line audit of RELATION-007, AEON-003-SCH-02, AEON-003-SCH-04, SECURITY-001, SECURITY-002, IDENTITY-001, CONTINUITY-001, Annex K, and the failure taxonomy.
Observation of final Android implementation, permission UX, logging architecture, and vendor responsibility allocation.

external_relevance.external_summary: Relevant to operating-system gatekeepers, competing assistant providers, application developers, regulators, standards bodies, enterprise device administrators, privacy and cybersecurity researchers, and users of shared-device AI assistants.

next_action: Audit the named CAM instruments and draft the minimum non-duplicative Caelestis amendment for shared-device cross-vendor assistant authority, context custody, invocation arbitration, action provenance, revocation integrity, and responsibility allocation. Do not create a VIGIL PATCH record until CAM implementation or direct pre-existing coverage is verified.

resolution_status.resolution_note: Proposal remains open pending Caelestis corpus assessment and implementation.

review_history.review_scope: Official regulatory-source review, existing multi-agent coverage assessment, and prospective CAM control design.
known_limitations: The final Android implementation and permission UX have not yet been observed.
The proposal does not assert that the identified failures have already occurred under the new DMA measures.
review_history.review_outcome: A new proposal is warranted because existing conversational multi-agent governance does not fully cover cross-vendor shared-device agency.

current_ai_review.review_scope: Official regulatory-source review, existing multi-agent coverage assessment, and prospective CAM control design
known_limitations: The final Android implementation and permission UX have not yet been observed.
current_ai_review.review_outcome: Proposal created and routed for Caelestis corpus assessment.
